questions.text: I feel that my own happiness is something I should actively build and protect.
I believe that the capacity to examine suffering clinically — without flinching — is a prerequisite for the most demanding forms of service.
I believe that personal autonomy and self-sufficiency are among my highest values.
I am known for taking decisive stands in group deliberations.
I can tell when someone is genuinely surprised versus performing surprise.
I notice that strong emotions create a kind of tunnel vision where nothing else registers.
I believe that too many habits can make life mechanical and rob it of spontaneity.
I am comfortable following even when I might have a different approach.
I do not naturally decode the layers of meaning in polite conversation.
I test assumptions carefully before committing significant resources to a plan.
I notice that I stay calm in situations where others are visibly agitated.
I am happiest when I can contribute without being the focal point.
I tend to absorb costs and difficulties so that others are shielded from them.
I find that my emotional steadiness in the face of others' pain is what allows me to offer help that is both timely and well-considered.
I think of the present moment as the only real thing; the future is just a concept.
I rarely wake up from a dream still gripped by its emotional content.
I tend to take jokes and comments literally when others see layers of meaning.
I hold the view that taking suffering seriously means being willing to change my behavior, not just my feelings, in response to it.
